titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, 24 August 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Bliss Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: Letter from sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender (1876-1960) to his wife, Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). In this letter, Spender lists the birthdays of himself (his pet-name is 'Wolf') and his family, but states that he cannot yet remember the dates of 'extraneous events' such as wedding anniversaries and Lillian's birthday. Spender writes that he has seen his name on a list, and suspects that it means he will soon be moved to a Division as he has long wanted, although he thinks a pity that he will lose the opportunity to finish his dispatch. He discusses the difficulty of translating the experience of war into the limited, inexpressive vocabulary he may use with the Press, and complains of the laziness of his typing clerk. He briefly mentions golf, relations among the officers, and the difficulty the army is having in recruiting outside Belfast due to Sinn Féin activity.Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender wrote a collection of letters to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Aug 25th 24 My pr A brief letter today only today, when
the b w was expecting to find 2 for him.
Poor bw. I have a good many other letters to
answer so shall make this my excuse for a
short one too. I can remember all the family birthdays
Rex's Sept 4 Mabs Sept 6 Mater Sept 22
? 21stWolfs Oct 6 Eds Oct 26th but it is the
extraneous events Eg engagement & wedding anni-
versaries pr's birthday etc which I have not
yet got into yet!
I could not help noticing my name on a
list with 2 others â presumably sent forward
as suggestions for appointment to a Division.
It is rather like references of a butler. In
some ways I should have liked to have finished
the dispatch first as I have now got into it,
but I am making very heavy weather of it
and perhaps it will be as well to introduce
fresh blood. Gen' B has been away & I
find the CGS a much more indulgent reader
of my daily efforts & get them passed in
a few minutes usually without comments,
whilst with B it has become a regular
jigsaw puzzle. I'm afraid the press might
not like the bow's classical spelling as much as
a pr so am very careful in my selection of
words, but how I hate the inexpressive
Artillery activity when I know what it
really means from actual experience. My typing clerk has been rather lazy
& I am being held up in consequence, very
annoying as I feel the days gaining on me
but I mean to have a real set to again
this afternoon so as to finish July by the
end of this! week. It would make it so
much easier to have a certain lady typist! I am amused at Greatmen's little foibles: â
Butler's I have described, Sir DH's is his golf
the one game thing at which he is perhaps rather behind
his contemporaries tho my no means a bad
player. They say that he would rather get
a reduction of 4 in his handicap than an
O M Garter or whatever particular decoration
he still lacks. Kiggell's simplicity is very striking he will
continually hold a young officer's horse whilst he
speaks to the Chief without thinking it in the
least infra dig. I hear from Douglas that
security in Belfast is improving but that outside
the Sinn Feiners discountenance any security
meetings! Your bw W B Spender
Written at the top of first letter page Douglas who has
been doing tea duty
in the mess is to do
4 days full duty in
place of another orderly
gone on leave. I thought
he was not quite keen
about it but think it
was only a little hesitation.
Certainly 4 days full
work will be a good
change as I think he
hasn't enough to keep
him occupied which it
is his nature to be.
